title: Meta Variants
html:
Meta Variants

Meta Variants

A meta variant is an internal character set that is used to translate between Unicode and a supported external character set.

Uniface processes data in Unicode, whereas data originating from, or destined for, the operating system, DBMS, or GUI is formatted using a supported external character. Incoming data is first converted to Unicode before being processed, and outgoing data is converted to the external character set.

To handle the conversion between Unicode and an external character set, Uniface loads another internal character set, known as a meta variant. Each meta variant is mapped to several external character sets. For example, the meta_Western variant is mapped to the CP1252 character set on Windows and the ISO 8859-1 on Unix, and the meta_Japanese is mapped to the Japanese Shift-JIS and EUC character sets.

When Uniface is started, it automatically loads the Unicode character set and the basic meta variant, which is meta_Western. In addition, it can load one additional meta variant. For example, if the Japanese Shift-JIS character set is already loaded, it is not possible to load the Chinese GB character set in the same session. However, because the Japanese Shift-JIS character set and the Japanese EUC character set are mapped to the same meta variant, it is possible to load these two character sets in the same Uniface session. Loading character sets mapped to meta_Western is always possible.

The external character set used is determined at runtime by the $sys_charset and $def_charset assignment settings, the installed character set, and the entity character set as determined by the entity properties.

Note: Uniface meta variant data can also be stored in the DBMS, so DBMS data stored using one meta variant should not be retrieved using another meta variant.

Setting Meta Variant in Translation Tables

In translation tables, the meta variant is identified using the Meta_Variant mnemonic.

User-defined translation tables created with previous versions of Uniface must be edited to add the meta variant definition, and then recompiled.

Once a meta variant has been set, you can not set it to something else in the same table. Statements that set the meta_Variant are:

  • meta_Variant TableType
  • CodePage ^charset
  • ^SJIS FONT_xx

For example, once you have set the meta variant to Unicode using Unicode ^printer you can not set CP1252 ^charset because the meta variant of CP1252 is not Unicode but meta_Western; you will get a compile error saying:

"conflicting Meta_Variant with Charset"

Once you set a codepage, you restrict the characters that can be output to the characters available in that character set. When you have the ^charset statement in your device table, each code point on the left in each mapping represents a code point in that character set. Uniface uses this character set to translate these code points to UTF16 when printing a character. If you do not provide ^charset, then the default character set is latin1.

Related Links
  • Installable Character Sets

    Character sets that conform to one of several characters encoding systems can be installed into Uniface applications.

  • Uniface Mappings to Installable Character Sets

    During Uniface installation, it is possible to choose a character set that is appropriate for one or more languages. Thus Western European is appropriate for English, German, French and so on, and Cyrillic is appropriate for Slavic languages such as Russian.
